Investor Anatomy
US Inflation and Price Dynamics
Home
US Inflation and Price Dynamics
Systematically synthesises realised inflation, cost pressures, and market expectations to assess the prevailing US inflation regime.

Regime Assessment:

  • Transitional / Fragile Balance
  • Medium Confidence
  • Fragile

Why This Regime:

  • High-confidence signals for PCE (1) and Market Implied Inflation (2) currently anchor the theme in a neutral state.
  • Dynamic weighting emphasizes these stable cyclical signals over weakening tactical momentum.
  • The US Inflation Signal was downweighted due to Low Confidence and internal conflicts between z-scores and regime labels (3).
  • The Wage Growth signal was downweighted due to Low Confidence and lagged ECI inputs (4).

Alignment & Tensions:

  • Policy-relevant inflation (1) and market expectations (2) reinforce a stable, mid-range regime.
  • Internal tension exists as the Term-Structure signal (5) and PPI/CPI divergence (6) trend toward reflationary/hot thresholds.
  • Tensions do not overturn the regime because the highest-weighted composite indicators remain within neutral bounds.

Scenario Balance:

  • Dominant scenario: Inflation expectations remain anchored while realized policy-relevant prices oscillate near historical norms (2).
  • Primary upside risk: Accelerating producer prices trigger a "Hot" regime shift via the divergence metric (6).
  • Primary downside risk: Continued rapid compression of the US Inflation composite z-score into a confirmed "Cool" state (3).

What Would Change the Regime:

  • A PCE_Composite move above 0.75 or below -0.75 would force a regime exit (1).
  • A Divergence z-score reaching 0.75 while CPI remains above 3.0% would trigger a "Hot" classification (6).

Model Lineage

Visualisations below present upstream signal states and evidence context used to form the current silver market interpretation.

Inflation Signal

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement (mandatory): Given a composite z-score of 0.0048 and a 10-year rolling average baseline, this setup aligns with Transition-biased price paths and Indeterminate volatility, where the dominant risk is Data uncertainty, not regime shift (1).
  • The signal has entered a transition state following a sharp decline from neutral levels, though provided labels suggest a disinflationary bias (1).
  • Conviction Band: Low; Interpretation Confidence: Low Confidence; Internal Conflict Flag: Yes. Signal Stability Assessment: Volatile; Threshold Proximity: Near; Revision Sensitivity: Moderate.

Methodology Applied:

  • A composite z-score between -0.25 and +0.25 indicates a TRANSITION environment at a turning point or directionless state (1).
  • Falling values imply disinflation, demand slowdown, or the effects of monetary tightening (1).
  • Regime COOL is strictly defined as a composite z-score <= -0.50, signifying disinflationary or deflationary pressure (1).
  • US Inflation Signal (latest observation: 2026-04-30) (1).

Key Dynamics:

  • The primary driver is the rapid compression of the composite z-score from 0.427 to 0.0048 in one month (1).
  • Momentum has shifted downward, suggesting a disinflationary impulse is currently overriding previous neutral stability (1).
  • Internal Conflict: The provided data labels the current state as "COOL" despite the z-score remaining above the -0.50 methodology threshold (1).
  • Conditional Invalidation: A reversal of the z-score above +0.25 would re-establish a Neutral regime (1).
  • Signal stability is poor, as the indicator has frequently alternated between Neutral and Cool labels over the trailing 12 months (1).

Scenario Balance:

  • Base case dominant: Continued disinflationary momentum leading to a confirmed "COOL" regime breach (<= -0.50).
  • Upside risk: Mean reversion to Neutral triggered by a spike in copper prices or industrial production (1).
  • Downside risk: Accelerated demand deceleration driving the composite deep into deflationary territory.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months). The signal is designed to inform macro asset allocation and risk budgeting over several months (1).
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Low, due to the conflict between numeric thresholds and regime labeling in the provided data (1).

Macro Relevance:

  • Informs aggregate latent inflation pressure across realized prices, market expectations, and liquidity proxies (1).
  • Implied mechanism: Disinflationary pressure typically supportive of monetary easing and duration-heavy positions (1).
  • Cycle position: Not determined.
  • Interacts with USD strength and M2 liquidity to confirm whether price moves are monetary or demand-driven (1).

Regime Context:

  • The signal is in a weakening transition, moving from a persistent Neutral state toward the Cool threshold (1).
  • Direction of change: Weakening.

Model Limitations:

  • CPI is subject to revisions and base effects, which may lag real-time price shifts (1).
  • TIPS liquidity can distort breakeven rates during periods of market stress (1).

Data & References:

  • US Inflation Signal: latest observation 2026-04-30 (1).
  • Influential Datapoints: Composite z-score (0.0048) and the recent downward trend from 0.427 (1).
  • Additional public datasets: Headline CPI (YoY) and 10Y Breakeven Inflation Rates for component verification.
Wage Growth and Inflation Signal

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement (mandatory): Given a Wage_Pressure_z of -0.53 and Labor_Tightness_z of -0.17 (1), this setup aligns with Range-biased price paths and Normal volatility, where the dominant risk is Mean reversion, not Regime shift.
  • The signal is currently in a NEUTRAL regime, indicating wage growth aligned with historical norms (1).
  • Conviction Band: Low; Interpretation Confidence: Low Confidence; Internal Conflict Flag: No. Signal Stability Assessment: Stable; Threshold Proximity: Moderate; Revision Sensitivity: Unknown.

Methodology Applied:

  • A z-score between -1.0 and +1.0 designates a NEUTRAL regime, implying a stable inflation outlook (1).
  • The Inflation Composite blends Wage Pressure and Labor Market Tightness (LMT) to predict core inflation persistence (1).
  • Weights are applied to Employment Cost Index (ECI) for private and civilian workers to compute pressure (1).
  • Wage Growth and Inflation Signal: observation date not provided.

Key Dynamics:

  • The primary driver is the convergence of Private and Civilian Wage YoY growth at 3.3% (1).
  • Stabilisation is evident as the Inflation Composite z-score has remained in the NEUTRAL band for fifteen consecutive observations (1).
  • Internal coherence is high as both wage and labor tightness metrics support a non-inflationary expansion.
  • Conditional Invalidation: An Inflation Composite z-score exceeding +1.0 (HOT regime) (1).
  • The signal exhibits high persistence, having maintained the NEUTRAL classification throughout the provided history.

Scenario Balance:

  • Base case dominant: Continued range-bound inflation as wage growth tracks the 24-month baseline (1).
  • Upside risk: A sudden spike in Labor_Tightness_z triggering a "HOT" wage-push inflation regime (3).
  • Downside risk: Rising labor market slack leading to a "COOL" disinflationary transition (1).

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months); ECI inputs are quarterly and reflect medium-term cost structures (1).
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Low; data timeliness cannot be assessed from provided content.

Macro Relevance:

  • Informs the "pricing" macro dimension by measuring the intensity of wage-push inflation (1).
  • Economic mechanism: Inflationary pressure originating from labor costs and supply-demand imbalances (1).
  • Cycle position: Not determined.
  • Typically confirms trends in Policy-Relevant Inflation (PCE) (2) and labor demand signals.

Regime Context:

  • Persistent NEUTRAL regime; no recent transitions observed in the Inflation Composite.
  • Direction of change: Stabilising within the neutral corridor.

Model Limitations:

  • ECI data is quarterly and lagged, requiring interpolation between releases (1).
  • JOLTS-based metrics (used for tightness) are subject to significant survey noise (1).

Data & References:

  • Wage Growth and Inflation Signal, latest observation date not provided (1).
  • Influential datapoints: Wage_Pressure_z (-0.53) and Labor_Tightness_z (-0.17).
  • Additional datasets: US CPI/PCE data (2) and PMI Prices would improve reliability.
Policy-Relevant Inflation (PCE) Signal

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement: Given a PCE_Composite of -0.277, CorePCE_YoY at 2.97%, and Z_cPCE at -0.312, this setup aligns with Range-biased price paths and Normal volatility, where the dominant risk is Mean reversion, not Regime shift. (1)
  • The signal is currently in a Neutral regime, characterized by inflation levels that have stabilized between the "Hot" and "Cool" thresholds. (1)
  • Conviction Band: Medium; Interpretation Confidence: High Confidence; Internal Conflict Flag: No. Signal Stability Assessment: Stable; Threshold Proximity: Moderate; Revision Sensitivity: High.

Methodology Applied:

  • PCE_Composite: A z-score between -0.75 and 0.75 defines a Neutral regime, suggesting inflation is near historical trends. (1)
  • Level_pp > 0: If YoY inflation exceeds the 2% target, it indicates structural pressure on the central bank to remain restrictive. (1)
  • Momentum Rule: Used to identify early warnings of tightening cycles when the composite score accelerates while in a Neutral state. (1)
  • Latest dataset observation: 2026-02-28. (1)

Key Dynamics:

  • The dominant driver is the stabilization of both Headline (2.80%) and Core (2.97%) PCE within the Neutral z-score range. (1)
  • Momentum is currently negligible, with the composite score drifting slightly from -0.236 to -0.277, indicating regime persistence. (1)
  • Internal coherence is high as both Z_PCE and Z_cPCE are currently negative and aligned within the same regime. (1)
  • Conditional Invalidation: A PCE_Composite move above 0.75 or below -0.75 would trigger a mandatory regime shift. (1)

Scenario Balance:

  • Base case dominant: Continued Neutral regime as inflation tracks close to historical trend volatility.
  • Upside risk: Energy or supply shocks driving the PCE_Composite toward the "Hot" threshold (>0.75).
  • Downside risk: Demand-side collapse forcing a transition to the "Cool" regime (<-0.75).

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months), as inflation regimes typically persist over several quarters. (1)
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Medium, reflecting a stable macro baseline that neither demands immediate tightening nor easing.

Macro Relevance:

  • Informs the "Pricing" macro dimension by measuring underlying inflation persistence relative to policy targets. (1)
  • Economic mechanism: The signal implies structural monetary restriction because YoY levels remain above the 2% policy target. (1)
  • Cycle position: Not determined.
  • Typically interacts with labor market data to calibrate Federal Reserve policy expectations.

Regime Context:

  • The Neutral regime is highly persistent, having been maintained since September 2023. (1)
  • Direction of change: Stabilising.

Model Limitations:

  • Subject to material BEA revisions and monthly reporting lags. (1)
  • Z-scores may be distorted by historical structural breaks in inflation volatility. (1)

Data & References:

  • Latest Data: PCEPI (129.449), PCEPILFE (128.859), PCE_YoY (2.80%), CorePCE_YoY (2.97%) as of Feb 2026. (1)
  • Most influential: PCE_Composite (-0.277). (1)
  • External datasets for depth: Dallas Fed Trimmed Mean PCE and 10Y Breakeven Inflation Rates. (1)
Inflation Term-Structure Signal

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement: Given a composite score of 0.251 driven by rising 5-year breakevens (Z_T5: 0.61) and stable 10-year breakevens (Z_T10: 0.13), this setup aligns with Range-biased price paths and Normal volatility, where the dominant risk is Trend continuation, not Regime shift (1).
  • The signal currently occupies a Neutral regime, having moved upward from a recent low of -0.168 in February 2026 (1).
  • Conviction Band: Medium; Interpretation Confidence: High Confidence; Internal Conflict Flag: No. Signal Stability Assessment: Improving; Threshold Proximity: Moderate; Revision Sensitivity: Unknown.

Methodology Applied:

  • Composite scores between -0.75 and 0.75 are classified as Neutral, serving as a weak conditioning variable for benchmark macro exposures (1).
  • Rising composite values signify a transition toward reflation and easier financial conditions (1).
  • Weights are applied to 5-year breakevens (0.4), 10-year breakevens (0.3), and 10-year real TIPS yields (0.3) (1).
  • Latest observation date: April 30, 2026 (1).

Key Dynamics:

  • The primary driver is the 5-year breakeven inflation rate (T5YIE), which rose to 2.56% (Z_T5: 0.61), indicating firming short-term inflation compensation (1).
  • Real yields (DFII10) remain slightly above the rolling norm (Z_mReal: -0.11), providing a modest offset to rising breakevens (1).
  • Momentum is positive, with the composite rising for two consecutive months, though it remains below the Reflation threshold of 0.75 (1).
  • Conditional Invalidation: An absolute composite move exceeding 0.75, triggering a regime shift to Reflation or Disinflation.

Scenario Balance:

  • Base case dominant: Persistence in Neutral regime as breakevens and real yields oscillate within historical bands.
  • Upside risk: A transition to Reflation triggered by a sharp rise in 5-year inflation expectations above recent Z-score peaks.
  • Downside risk: A shift toward Disinflation driven by a spike in 10-year real yields (DFII10) or collapsing breakevens.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months) based on the monthly resampling and rolling 5-year historical logic (1).
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Medium; while the signal is Neutral, the positive trajectory provides a secondary tilt toward reflationary assets.

Macro Relevance:

  • Informs the pricing and liquidity dimension by measuring market-implied inflation compensation versus real financial tightening (1).
  • Economic mechanism: The current rising trend implies a gradual easing of real financial conditions through higher inflation expectations (1).
  • Cycle position: Not determined by methodology.
  • Interacts with commodity price trends to confirm if breakeven moves are supported by physical market demand (1).

Regime Context:

  • The Neutral regime has been persistent since May 2023, with no breaches of the 0.75 thresholds in the provided history (1).
  • Direction of change: Strengthening towards the Reflation boundary (1).

Model Limitations:

  • TIPS liquidity premia can distort breakevens during periods of market stress (1).
  • The signal is a relative measure due to its rolling window logic, rather than an absolute level indicator (1).

Data & References:

  • Inflation Term-Structure Signal (Latest: 2026-04-30) (1).
  • Influential datapoints: T5YIE (2.56) and DFII10 (1.93) (1).
  • External survey-based inflation expectations and commodity price indices would improve signal validation (1).
Market Implied Inflation Signal

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement: Given the 5-year breakeven (T5YIE) at 2.56 and 10-year breakeven (T10YIE) at 2.36 resulting in a composite z-score of 0.46, this setup aligns with Range-biased price paths and Normal volatility, where the dominant risk is Mean reversion, not Regime shift (1).
  • The signal is currently classified in the STABLE regime, reflecting anchored inflation expectations (1).
  • Conviction Band: Medium; Interpretation Confidence: High Confidence; Internal Conflict Flag: No. Signal Stability Assessment: Stable; Threshold Proximity: Moderate; Revision Sensitivity: Unknown.

Methodology Applied:

  • Expectations are RISING if the composite z-score ≥ 1.0, implying accelerating inflation risk premia (1).
  • Expectations are FALLING if z-score ≤ -0.5, implying disinflation or flight-to-quality (1).
  • Values between -0.5 and 1.0 are STABLE, indicating expectations are anchored within historical norms (1).
  • Latest observation date: 2026-04-30 (1).

Key Dynamics:

  • The primary driver is the T5YIE level z-score (0.76) and T10YIE level z-score (0.45), both remaining within neutral bounds (1).
  • Momentum metrics (yoy and 3-month annualized) show slight stabilization after a period of volatility in early 2025 (1).
  • Conditional Invalidation: A composite z-score move above 1.0 would shift the signal to RISING (1).
  • The signal has shown high persistence, remaining in the STABLE regime for over 35 months (1).

Scenario Balance:

  • Dominant base case: Anchored inflation expectations supporting neutral monetary policy pressure.
  • Upside risk: A breakout in breakevens leading to higher inflation risk premia.
  • Downside risk: Liquidity-driven compression of inflation compensation or disinflationary shocks.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months) as the signal tracks medium-term inflation compensation trends (1).
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Medium, as the signal is currently in a neutral, non-extreme state (1).

Macro Relevance:

  • Informs the pricing of inflation risk premia and the spread between nominal and real yields (1).
  • Economic mechanism: Measures the market's demand for inflation protection relative to nominal safety (1).
  • Cycle position: Not determined.
  • Interacts with Fed Inflation Signals and realized CPI/PPI to identify market leads or lags (1).

Regime Context:

  • The STABLE regime is highly persistent, showing long-term directional consistency (1).
  • Direction of change: Stabilising after a minor uptick in early 2026 (1).

Model Limitations:

  • Distorted by TIPS liquidity during market stress and sensitive to Treasury supply shifts (1).
  • Embeds risk premia rather than providing a pure inflation forecast (1).

Data & References:

  • T5YIE (2.56) and T10YIE (2.36) as of 2026-04-30 (1).
  • Most influential: Composite MIIE_z score of 0.46 (1).
  • Public datasets: 5-Year 5-Year Forward Inflation Expectation Rate (T5YIFR) and CPI/PPI realized data.
CPI vs PPI Divergence Signal

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement: Given a rising divergence z-score of 0.64 and CPI YoY at 3.29%, this setup aligns with Range-biased price paths and Normal volatility, where the dominant risk is Regime shift, not Mean reversion. (1)
  • The signal currently occupies a NEUTRAL regime, though it is rapidly approaching the HOT threshold due to accelerating producer prices. (1)
  • Conviction Band: Medium; Interpretation Confidence: High Confidence; Internal Conflict Flag: No. Signal Stability Assessment: Volatile; Threshold Proximity: Near; Revision Sensitivity: Unknown.

Methodology Applied:

  • A HOT regime is triggered if the divergence z-score (z) is ≥ 1.0, or if z ≥ 0.75 while CPI YoY remains ≥ 3.0%. (1)
  • The NEUTRAL regime, defined by an absence of extreme divergence, implies stable price relationships and no strong directional bias. (1)
  • Rising values in the "PPI minus CPI" spread imply increasing inflationary pressure and potential late-cycle expansion. (1)
  • CPI vs PPI Divergence Signal (latest observation: 2026-03-31). (1)

Key Dynamics:

  • The primary driver is the sharp acceleration in PPI YoY (6.03%) relative to CPI YoY (3.29%), lifting the z-score from 0.09 to 0.64 in two months. (1)
  • This represents a significant inflection toward upstream cost-push pressure, although it remains within the methodology's neutral band. (1)
  • Conditional Invalidation: The signal transitions to HOT if the z-score reaches 0.75 while CPI remains above 3.0%. (1)
  • The signal is currently volatile, ending a period of stability that persisted since late 2023. (1)

Scenario Balance:

  • Base case dominant: Continued NEUTRAL regime as the divergence has not yet breached the 0.75/1.0 thresholds.
  • Upside risk: Transition to HOT triggered by continued commodity or energy price spikes leading to PPI outperformance.
  • Downside risk: Mean reversion of the z-score if upstream costs decelerate faster than retail prices.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months), reflecting the lead-indicator properties of pipeline inflation.
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Medium, as the signal is in transition but lacks a confirmed regime flip.

Macro Relevance:

  • Informs the pricing dimension by identifying pipeline inflation pressures that precede retail price changes. (1)
  • Implies a potential margin compression mechanism as producer costs rise faster than consumer prices. (1)
  • Cycle position: Not determined.
  • Interacts with ISM Prices Paid and Inflation Breakevens to confirm the sustainability of upstream trends. (1)

Regime Context:

  • The signal has been persistent in the NEUTRAL regime since October 2023. (1)
  • Direction of change: Strengthening toward the HOT threshold. (1)

Model Limitations:

  • PPI data is subject to multi-month revisions which can introduce noise into single-month regime assessments. (1)
  • Pass-through from producer to consumer levels is often lagged and may be incomplete. (1)

Data & References:

  • CPI vs PPI Divergence Signal: Latest observation 2026-03-31. (1)
  • Most influential datapoints: PPI YoY (6.03%) and Divergence Z-score (0.64). (1)
  • Public datasets: ISM Prices Paid and Inflation Breakevens would improve depth.
Investor Anatomy

All views expressed are personal, based on publicly available information, and do not represent the views of any employer or reflect any proprietary or internal analysis. This information should not be relied upon for making investment decisions.
